Among the estimated 23,000 temples in Varanasi,[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBansal200848–49-43) the temples most popular for worship are: the Kashi Vishwanath Temple of Shiva; the [Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple](/wiki/Sankat_Mochan_Hanuman_Temple "Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple"); and the Durga Temple, known for monkeys that reside in the large trees nearby.[[74]](#cite_note-Varun-79)[[169]](#cite_note-temple-174)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECunninghamSastri2002131–140-37)

* **The [Kashi Vishwanath Temple](/wiki/Kashi_Vishwanath_Temple "Kashi Vishwanath Temple")**, on the Ganges, is one of the 12 *[Jyotirlinga](/wiki/Jyotirlinga "Jyotirlinga")* Shiva temples in Varanasi.[[169]](#cite_note-temple-174) The temple has been destroyed and rebuilt several times throughout its existence. The [Gyanvapi Mosque](/wiki/Gyanvapi_Mosque "Gyanvapi Mosque"), which is adjacent to the temple, is the original site of the temple.[[170]](#cite_note-Warrier2014-175) The temple, which is also known as the Golden Temple,[[171]](#cite_note-route-176) was built in 1780 by Queen [Ahilyabai Holkar](/wiki/Ahilyabai_Holkar "Ahilyabai Holkar") of [Indore](/wiki/Indore "Indore"). The two [pinnacles](/wiki/Shikara "Shikara") of the temple are covered in gold and were donated in 1839 by [Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh"), the ruler of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_(region) "Punjab (region)"). The dome is scheduled to receive gold plating through a proposed initiative of the Ministry of Culture and Religious Affairs of Uttar Pradesh. Numerous rituals, prayers, and [aartis](/wiki/Aarti "Aarti") are held daily at the temple between 02:30 and 23:00.[[172]](#cite_note-177)
* **The [Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple](/wiki/Sankat_Mochan_Hanuman_Temple "Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple")**, which is situated by the Asi River, is one of the sacred temples of the Hindu god [Hanuman](/wiki/Hanuman "Hanuman").[[173]](#cite_note-178) The present temple was built in the early 1900s by the educationist and [Indian independence figure](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement"), Pandit [Madan Mohan Malaviya](/wiki/Madan_Mohan_Malaviya "Madan Mohan Malaviya"), the founder of Banaras Hindu University.[[174]](#cite_note-ex-179) According to Hindu legend the temple was built on the spot where the medieval Hindu saint [Tulsidas](/wiki/Tulsidas "Tulsidas") had a vision of [Hanuman](/wiki/Hanuman "Hanuman").[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECallewaert200090-180) During a 7 March 2006 terrorist attack, one of three explosions hit the temple while a wedding was in progress, and resulted in injuries to 30 people apart from 23 deaths.[[174]](#cite_note-ex-179) Following the attack, a permanent police post was installed inside the temple.[[176]](#cite_note-181)
* **There are two temples dedicated to the goddess Durga in Varanasi**: [Durga Mandir](/wiki/Durga_Mandir,_Varanasi "Durga Mandir, Varanasi") built in the 16th century (exact date not known), and Durga Kund (Sanskrit 'kund' meaning "pond or pool") built in the 18th century. A large number of Hindu devotees visit Durga Kund during [Navratri](/wiki/Navratri "Navratri") to worship the goddess [Durga](/wiki/Durga "Durga"). The temple, built in the [Nagara](/wiki/Nagara_architecture "Nagara architecture") architectural style, has multi-tiered spires[[171]](#cite_note-route-176) and is stained red with [ochre](/wiki/Ochre "Ochre"), representing the red colour of Durga. The building has a rectangular tank of water called the [Durga Kund](/wiki/Durga_Mandir,_Varanasi "Durga Mandir, Varanasi") ("Kund" meaning a pond or pool). During annual celebrations of [Nag Panchami](/wiki/Nag_Panchami "Nag Panchami"), the act of depicting the god Vishnu reclining on the serpent [Shesha](/wiki/Shesha "Shesha") is recreated in the Kund.[[177]](#cite_note-182) While the [Annapurna Temple](/wiki/Annapurna_Devi_Mandir "Annapurna Devi Mandir"), located nearby to the Kashi Vishwanath Temple, is dedicated to [Annapoorna devi](/wiki/Annapoorna_devi "Annapoorna devi"), the goddess of food,[[169]](#cite_note-temple-174) the Sankatha Temple adjacent to the Sindhia Ghat is dedicated to Sankatha, the goddess of remedy. The Sankatha Temple has a large sculpture of a lion and a cluster of nine smaller temples dedicated to the [nine planets](/wiki/Navagraha "Navagraha").[[169]](#cite_note-temple-174)
* **[Parshvanath Jain temple](/wiki/Parshvanath_Jain_temple,_Varanasi "Parshvanath Jain temple, Varanasi")** is the temple of Jain religion dedicated to Parshvanath, the 23rd Thirthankara who was born at Bhelpur in Varanasi. The idol deified in the temple is of black colour and 75 centimetres (30 inches) in height. It is located in Bhelapur about 5 kilometres (3.1 miles) from the centre of Varanasi city and 3 kilometres (1.9 miles) from the Benares Hindu University. It belongs to the Digambar sect of Jainism and is a holy tirtha or pilgrimage centre for Jains.
* Other temples of note are: the [Bharat Mata Mandir](/wiki/Bharat_Mata_Mandir "Bharat Mata Mandir"), dedicated to the national personification of India, which was inaugurated by [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi") in 1936, the Kalabhairav Temple, the Mrithyunjay Mahadev Temple, and the [New Vishwanath Temple](/wiki/New_Vishwanath_Temple "New Vishwanath Temple") located in the campus of [BHU](/wiki/Banaras_Hindu_University "Banaras Hindu University"), the [Tulsi Manas Mandir](/wiki/Tulsi_Manas_Mandir "Tulsi Manas Mandir").[[169]](#cite_note-temple-174)

### Mosques

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Alamgir_Mosque_by_the_Ganges_ghats%2C_Varanasi.jpg/250px-Alamgir_Mosque_by_the_Ganges_ghats%2C_Varanasi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alamgir_Mosque_by_the_Ganges_ghats,_Varanasi.jpg)

[Alamgiri Mosque](/wiki/Alamgir_Mosque,_Varanasi "Alamgir Mosque, Varanasi")

There are 15 mosques of significant historical value in Varanasi. Of particular note are the Abdul Razzaq, Alamgir, Bibi Razia, Chaukhambha, Dhai Nim Kangore, Fatman, Ganje Shahada, Gyanavapi, and Hazrat Sayyed Salar Masud Dargah. Many of these mosques were constructed from the components of the Hindu shrines which were destroyed under the auspices of subsequent Muslim invaders or rulers. The two such well known mosques are the [Gyanvapi Mosque](/wiki/Gyanvapi_Mosque "Gyanvapi Mosque") and the [Alamgir Mosque](/wiki/Alamgir_Mosque "Alamgir Mosque").[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOut2010259-183)

The Gyanvapi Mosque was built by the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb in 1664 CE, after destroying a Hindu temple.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDesai200323-184) *Gyan Vapi* (Sanskrit: "the well of knowledge"), the name of the mosque, is derived from a well of the same name located within the precincts of the mosque.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitra2002[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidNviJaunGDJMCpgPA95_95]-185) The remains of an erstwhile temple can be seen in the foundation, the columns and at the rear part of the mosque.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBettsMcCulloch2013213-186) The façade of the mosque is modelled partially on the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal")'s entrance.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsher1992278-187) The mosque is administered by the Anjuman Inthazamiya Masajid (AIM).[[183]](#cite_note-188)

The Alamgiri Mosque was built in the 17th century by Aurangzeb over the ruins of a Hindu temple known as Bindu Madhav Temple.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrowtherRajWheeler1984-189) The temple that was destroyed was dedicated to [Vishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu") in the form of Bindu Madhav and had been built by Beni Madhavrao Scindia, a Maratha chieftain from [Gwalior](/wiki/Gwalior "Gwalior"). When emperor Aurangzeb had captured Banaras, he had ordered total destruction of all Hindu temples there. Aurangzeb then built a mosque over the ruins of this temple in 1669[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunlopSykesJackson2001135-190) and named it as Alamagir Mosque in the name of his own honorific title "Alamgir" which he had adopted after becoming the emperor of Mughal empire.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKumar200390-191)[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBettsMcCulloch2013213-186) The mosque is located at a prominent site above the [Panchganga Ghat](/w/index.php?title=Panchganga_Ghat&action=edit&redlink=1 "Panchganga Ghat (page does not exist)"), which is a funerary ghat facing the Ganges.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHussain1999342-192) The mosque is architecturally a blend of Islamic and Hindu architecture, particularly because of the lower part of the walls of the mosque having been built fully with the remains of the Hindu temple.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKumar200390-191) The mosque has high domes and minarets.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShetty201473-193)[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBettsMcCulloch2013213-186) Two of its minarets had been damaged; one minaret crashed killing a few people and the other minaret was officially brought down because of stability concerns.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBettsMcCulloch2013213-186) Non-Muslims are not allowed to enter the mosque.[[189]](#cite_note-194) The mosque has a security cordon of a police force.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVit-Suzan201411-195)

### Shri Guru Ravidass Janam Asthan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/RavidasiVaranasi.JPG/250px-RavidasiVaranasi.JPG)](/wiki/File:RavidasiVaranasi.JPG)

Memorial of [Sant Ravidas](/wiki/Sant_Ravidas "Sant Ravidas") at [Sant Ravidas Ghat](/wiki/Sant_Ravidas_Ghat "Sant Ravidas Ghat")

[Shri Guru Ravidass Janam Asthan](/wiki/Shri_Guru_Ravidass_Janam_Asthan "Shri Guru Ravidass Janam Asthan"), at [Sir Gobardhan](/wiki/Sir_Gobardhan "Sir Gobardhan") is the ultimate place of pilgrimage or religious headquarters for followers of the [Ravidassia religion](/wiki/Ravidassia_religion "Ravidassia religion").[[191]](#cite_note-outlookindia.com_2015-196) The foundation stone was laid on 14 June 1965 on *Ashad Sankranti* day at the birthplace of Ravidas. The temple was completed in 1994.[[192]](#cite_note-197)

### Sarnath

[Sarnath](/wiki/Sarnath "Sarnath") is located 10 kilometres north-east of Varanasi near the confluence of the Ganges and the [Varuna](/wiki/Varuna "Varuna") rivers in [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh"), India.

The deer park in Sarnath is where [Gautama Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha") first taught the [Dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma"), and where the Buddhist [Sangha](/wiki/Sangha "Sangha") came into existence through the [enlightenment](/wiki/Enlightenment_in_Buddhism "Enlightenment in Buddhism") of [Kondanna](/wiki/Kondanna_Buddha "Kondanna Buddha"),[[193]](#cite_note-bn-198) as described by the [Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta](/wiki/Dhammacakkappavattana_Sutta "Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta").

The city is mentioned by the Buddha as one of the [four places of pilgrimage](/wiki/Buddhist_pilgrimage_sites#Four_main_pilgrimage_sites_listed_by_Buddha_himself "Buddhist pilgrimage sites") which his devout followers should visit.[[194]](#cite_note-199)

## Culture

### Literature

See also: [The City of Varanasi in Literature](/wiki/The_City_of_Varanasi_in_Literature "The City of Varanasi in Literature")

Renowned Indian writers who have resided in the city were Kabir, Ravidas, and Tulsidas, who wrote much of his *Ram Charit Manas* here. Kulluka Bhatt wrote the best known account of *[Manusmriti](/wiki/Manusmriti "Manusmriti")* in Varanasi in the 15th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Later writers of the city have included [Acharya Shukla](/wiki/Ramchandra_Shukla "Ramchandra Shukla"), [Baldev Upadhyaya](/wiki/Baldev_Upadhyaya "Baldev Upadhyaya"), [Bharatendu Harishchandra](/wiki/Bharatendu_Harishchandra "Bharatendu Harishchandra"), [Devaki Nandan Khatri](/wiki/Devaki_Nandan_Khatri "Devaki Nandan Khatri"), Premchand, [Hazari Prasad Dwivedi](/wiki/Hazari_Prasad_Dwivedi "Hazari Prasad Dwivedi"), [Jaishankar Prasad](/wiki/Jaishankar_Prasad "Jaishankar Prasad"), [Kshetresa Chandra Chattopadhyaya](/wiki/Kshetresa_Chandra_Chattopadhyaya "Kshetresa Chandra Chattopadhyaya"), Sudama Pandey (Dhoomil), [Vagish Shastri](/wiki/Vagish_Shastri "Vagish Shastri"), and [Vidya Niwas Mishra](/wiki/Vidya_Niwas_Mishra "Vidya Niwas Mishra").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Several newspapers and journals are or were published in Varanasi such as *Varanasi Chandroday* and its successor *Kashivartaprakashika*, which became a weekly journal, first published on 1 June 1851.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMedhasananda2002653-200) The main newspaper is *[Aj](/wiki/Aj_(newspaper) "Aj (newspaper)")*, a Hindi-language nationalist newspaper first published in 1920.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKasbekar2006126-201) The newspaper was the bulwark of the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") and is a major newspaper of Hindi northern India.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKasbekar2006126-201)

### Art

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Wall_paintings%2C_Varanasi%2C_1973.jpg/250px-Wall_paintings%2C_Varanasi%2C_1973.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wall_paintings,_Varanasi,_1973.jpg)

[Wall paintings](/wiki/Wall_painting "Wall painting"), Varanasi, 1973

Varanasi is a major centre of arts and designs. It is a producer of silks and brocades with gold and silver thread work, carpet weaving, wooden toys, bangles made of glass, ivory work, perfumes, artistic brass and copper ware and a variety of handicrafts.[[197]](#cite_note-topic-202)[[198]](#cite_note-bhu-203) The cantonment graveyard of the British Raj is now the location of Varanasi's Arts and Crafts.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETiwari20109-204)

Notable artists (musicians and dancers) and historians who are connected with the city include Thakur Jaidev Singh, Mahadev Prasad Mishra, [Bismillah Khan](/wiki/Bismillah_Khan "Bismillah Khan"), [Ravi Shankar](/wiki/Ravi_Shankar "Ravi Shankar"), [Girija Devi](/wiki/Girija_Devi "Girija Devi"), [Gopal Shankar Misra](/wiki/Gopal_Shankar_Misra "Gopal Shankar Misra"), [Gopi Krishna](/wiki/Gopi_Krishna_(dancer) "Gopi Krishna (dancer)"), [Kishan Maharaj](/wiki/Kishan_Maharaj "Kishan Maharaj"), [Lalmani Misra](/wiki/Lalmani_Misra "Lalmani Misra"), Premlata Sharma, [N. Rajam](/wiki/N._Rajam "N. Rajam"), [Siddheshwari Devi](/wiki/Siddheshwari_Devi "Siddheshwari Devi"), [Samta Prasad](/wiki/Samta_Prasad "Samta Prasad"), [Sitara Devi](/wiki/Sitara_Devi "Sitara Devi"),[[200]](#cite_note-205) [Chhannulal Mishra](/wiki/Chhannulal_Mishra "Chhannulal Mishra"), Rajan Sajan Mishra, Ritwik Sanyal, Soma Ghosh, Devashish Dey, Ramkrishna Das and Harish Tiwari.

### Music

Main article: [Music in Varanasi](/wiki/Music_in_Varanasi "Music in Varanasi")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Goswami_Tulsidas_Awadhi_Hindi_Poet.jpg/250px-Goswami_Tulsidas_Awadhi_Hindi_Poet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goswami_Tulsidas_Awadhi_Hindi_Poet.jpg)

Sant Goswami Tulsidas Awadhi, a Hindi poet and propagator of Bhakthi music in Varanasi

Varanasi's music tradition is traced to the [Pauranic](/wiki/Puranas "Puranas") days. According to ancient legend, Shiva is credited with evolving music and dance forms. During the medieval era, [Vaishnavism](/wiki/Vaishnavism "Vaishnavism"), a [Bhakti movement](/wiki/Bhakti_movement "Bhakti movement"), grew in popularity, and Varanasi became a thriving centre for musicians such as [Surdas](/wiki/Surdas "Surdas"), [Kabir](/wiki/Kabir "Kabir"), [Ravidas](/wiki/Ravidas "Ravidas"), [Meera](/wiki/Meera "Meera") and Tulsidas. During the monarchic rule of Govind Chandra in the 16th century, the [Dhrupad](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad") style of singing received royal patronage and led to other related forms of music such as Dhamar, Hori, and Chaturang. Presently the Dhrupad maestro Pandit [Ritwik Sanyal](/wiki/Ritwik_Sanyal "Ritwik Sanyal") from Varanasi is working for the revival of this art-music.[[201]](#cite_note-Music-206)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Hemanta_Mukherjee_with_Rajendra_Prasad_and_Jwaharlal_Nehru%2C_1950.JPG/250px-Hemanta_Mukherjee_with_Rajendra_Prasad_and_Jwaharlal_Nehru%2C_1950.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hemanta_Mukherjee_with_Rajendra_Prasad_and_Jwaharlal_Nehru,_1950.JPG)

Hemant Kumar with Rajendra Prasad and Jawaharlal Nehru in 1950

The legend Bollywood singer [Hemant Kumar](/wiki/Hemant_Kumar "Hemant Kumar") or, Hemanta Mukherjee known as "Voice Of God" was born in Benaras.
In recent times, Girija Devi, the native famous classical singer of [thumris](/wiki/Thumri "Thumri"), was widely appreciated and respected for her musical renderings.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroughtonEllingham200091-207) Varanasi is also associated with many great instrumentalists such as [Bismillah Khan](/wiki/Bismillah_Khan "Bismillah Khan")[[201]](#cite_note-Music-206) and Pandit [Ravi Shankar](/wiki/Ravi_Shankar "Ravi Shankar"), the famous [sitar](/wiki/Sitar "Sitar") player and musicologist who was given the highest civilian award of the country, the [Bharat Ratna](/wiki/Bharat_Ratna "Bharat Ratna").[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBruynBainAllardice2010470-208) Varanasi has joined the global bandwagon of UNESCO "Cities of Music" under the Creative Cities Network.[[204]](#cite_note-209)

### Festivals

On [Maha Shivaratri](/wiki/Maha_Shivaratri "Maha Shivaratri") (February), a procession of Shiva proceeds from the Mahamrityunjaya Temple to the Kashi Vishwanath Temple.[[74]](#cite_note-Varun-79) [Dhrupad Mela](/wiki/Dhrupad_Mela "Dhrupad Mela") is a five-day musical festival devoted to [dhrupad](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad") style held at Tulsi Ghat in February–March.[[205]](#cite_note-festival1-210) The [Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple](/wiki/Sankat_Mochan_Hanuman_Temple "Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple") celebrates [Hanuman Jayanti](/wiki/Hanuman_Jayanti "Hanuman Jayanti") (March–April), the birthday of Hanuman. A special [puja](/wiki/Puja_(Hinduism) "Puja (Hinduism)"), [aarti](/wiki/Aarti "Aarti"), and a public procession is organised.[[206]](#cite_note-sm-211)[[207]](#cite_note-212) Since 1923, the temple has organised a five-day classical music and dance concert festival named *[Sankat Mochan Sangeet Samaroh](/wiki/Sankat_Mochan_Sangeet_Samaroh "Sankat Mochan Sangeet Samaroh")*, wherein iconic artists from all parts of India are invited to perform.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Statue_of_Durga%2C_Ganges_Ghats%2C_Varanasi%2C_India_-_October_2014.jpg/250px-Statue_of_Durga%2C_Ganges_Ghats%2C_Varanasi%2C_India_-_October_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Durga,_Ganges_Ghats,_Varanasi,_India_-_October_2014.jpg)

Durga Puja idol Visarjan in 2014

The [Durga Puja](/wiki/Durga_Puja "Durga Puja") celebrated by bengalis of Varanasi in a large form.A centuries-old Durga idol housed in Purana Durga Bari, installed in 1767 by Kali Prasanna Mukhopadhyay.The [Bharat Sevashram Sangha](/wiki/Bharat_Sevashram_Sangha "Bharat Sevashram Sangha") was founded in 1939,which is celebrating Durga Puja from decades.The famous [Durga Kund Mandir](/wiki/Durga_Kund_Mandir "Durga Kund Mandir") was constructed by [Rani Bhabani](/wiki/Rani_Bhabani "Rani Bhabani") of Natore is another place for devotion of Durga. Along with several local bengali cultural clubs performs several functions like jatra (folk theatre) singing competitions, dhunuchi nach, dance programs performed by the girls along with free Bhandaras for five days in several puja pandals. Volunteers majorly helps, present in every pandal and the celebration happens grandly.

The [Ramlila](/wiki/Ramlila "Ramlila") of [Ramnagar](/wiki/Ramnagar,_Varanasi "Ramnagar, Varanasi") is a dramatic enactment of Rama's legend, as told in *Ramacharitamanasa*.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitra2002216-77) The plays, sponsored by Kashi Naresh, are performed in Ramnagar every evening for 31 days.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitra2002216-77) On the last day, the festivities reach a crescendo as Rama vanquishes the demon king [Ravana](/wiki/Ravana "Ravana").[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitra2002216-77) Kashi Naresh [Udit Narayan Singh](/wiki/Udit_Narayan_Singh "Udit Narayan Singh") started this tradition around 1830.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitra2002216-77)

[Chhath Puja](/wiki/Chhath "Chhath") is celebrated on the sixth day of the lunar month of [Kartika](/wiki/K%C4%81rtika_(month) "Kārtika (month)") (October–November).[[208]](#cite_note-213)[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESingh2009221-214)[[210]](#cite_note-215) The rituals are observed over four days.[[211]](#cite_note-216) They include holy bathing, fasting and abstaining from drinking water (vrata), standing in water, and offering prasad (prayer offerings) and arghya to the setting and rising sun.[[212]](#cite_note-217) Some devotees also perform a prostration march as they head for the river banks. Chhath puja is dedicated to the sun god "[Surya](/wiki/Surya "Surya")" and his sister "[Chhathi Maiya](/wiki/Chhath "Chhath")".[[213]](#cite_note-218) Chhath is considered as Mahaparva by the [Bhojpuri people](/wiki/Bhojpuri_people "Bhojpuri people").[[214]](#cite_note-219)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Nag_Nathaiya_festival_in_Varanasi.jpg/250px-Nag_Nathaiya_festival_in_Varanasi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nag_Nathaiya_festival_in_Varanasi.jpg)

An actor reenacting [Krishna](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna") standing on the serpent [Kaliya](/wiki/Kaliya "Kaliya"), during the [Nag Nathaiya](/wiki/Nag_Nathaiya_(Festival) "Nag Nathaiya (Festival)") festival in Varanasi

[Nag Nathaiya](/wiki/Nag_Nathaiya "Nag Nathaiya") is celebrated on the fourth lunar day of the dark fortnight of the Hindu month of [Kartik](/wiki/Kartik_(month) "Kartik (month)") (October–November). It commemorates the victory of [Krishna](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna") over the serpent [Kaliya](/wiki/Kaliya "Kaliya"). On this occasion, a large [Kadamba tree](/wiki/Neolamarckia_cadamba "Neolamarckia cadamba") (*Neolamarckia cadamba*) branch is planted on the banks of the Ganges so that a boy, playing the role of Krishna, can jump into the river on to the effigy representing Kaliya. He stands over the effigy in a dancing pose playing the [flute](/wiki/Flute "Flute"), while an audience watches from the banks of the river or from boats.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPintchman200569–70-220) *[Bharat Milap](/wiki/Bharat_Milap "Bharat Milap")* celebrates the meeting of Rama and his younger brother [Bharata](/wiki/Bharata_(Ramayana) "Bharata (Ramayana)") after the return of the former after 14 years of exile.[[74]](#cite_note-Varun-79) It is celebrated during October–November, a day after the festival of [Vijayadashami](/wiki/Vijayadashami "Vijayadashami"). Kashi Naresh attends this festival in his regal attire. The festival attracts a large number of devotees.[[216]](#cite_note-festival-221)

*Ganga Mahotsav* is a five-day music festival organised by the Uttar Pradesh Tourism Department, held in November–December. It culminates a day before [Kartik Purnima](/wiki/Kartik_Purnima "Kartik Purnima"), also called [the Ganges festival](/wiki/Dev_Deepawali_(Varanasi) "Dev Deepawali (Varanasi)"). On this occasion the Ganges is attended by thousands of pilgrims, release lighted lamps to float in the river from the ghats.[[74]](#cite_note-Varun-79)[[205]](#cite_note-festival1-210)

The primary Muslim festivals celebrated annually in the city are the *[Eid al-Fitr](/wiki/Eid_al-Fitr "Eid al-Fitr")*, *[Bakrid](/wiki/Eid_al-Adha "Eid al-Adha")*, [mid-*Sha'ban*](/wiki/Mid-Sha%27ban "Mid-Sha'ban"), *Bara Wafat* and *[Muharram](/wiki/Muharram "Muharram")*. Additional festivals include *Alvida* and *[Chehlum](/wiki/Arba%27een "Arba'een")*. A non-religious festival observed by Muslims is *Ghazi-Miyan-ka-byaha* ("the marriage of *Ghazi Miyan*").[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESukul1974262-222)[[218]](#cite_note-223)

### Cuisine

In 2019, the sale of meat was banned within 250 meters of all Varanasi temples and heritage sites.[[219]](#cite_note-224) In 2025, the sale of all meat was banned in Varanasi during [Navaratri](/wiki/Navaratri "Navaratri").[[220]](#cite_note-225)

## Education

Main article: [Educational institutions in Varanasi](/wiki/List_of_educational_institutions_in_Varanasi "List of educational institutions in Varanasi")

[![The Banaras Hindu University in Varanasi](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/BHU_Main_Gate%2C_Banaras_Hindu_University_enhanced.jpg/250px-BHU_Main_Gate%2C_Banaras_Hindu_University_enhanced.jpg)](/wiki/File:BHU_Main_Gate,_Banaras_Hindu_University_enhanced.jpg)

The Banaras Hindu University in Varanasi

Historically, Varanasi has been a centre for education in India, attracting students and scholars from across the country.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma1995191-226)[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGupta200641-227) Varanasi has an overall literacy rate of 80% (male literacy: 85%, female literacy: 75%).[[95]](#cite_note-uastats-100) It is home to a [number of colleges and universities](/wiki/List_of_educational_institutions_in_Varanasi "List of educational institutions in Varanasi"). Most notably, it is the site of [Banaras Hindu University](/wiki/Banaras_Hindu_University "Banaras Hindu University") (BHU), which is one of the largest residential universities in Asia with over 20,000 students.[[223]](#cite_note-228) The [Indian Institute of Technology (BHU) Varanasi](/wiki/Indian_Institute_of_Technology_(BHU)_Varanasi "Indian Institute of Technology (BHU) Varanasi") is designated an [Institute of National Importance](/wiki/List_of_Institutes_of_National_Importance "List of Institutes of National Importance") and is one of 16 [Indian Institutes of Technology](/wiki/Indian_Institutes_of_Technology "Indian Institutes of Technology"). Other colleges and universities in Varanasi include [Jamia-e-Imania](/wiki/Jamia-e-Imania "Jamia-e-Imania"), the Institute of Integrated Management and Technology, [Mahatma Gandhi Kashi Vidyapith](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi_Kashi_Vidyapith "Mahatma Gandhi Kashi Vidyapith"), [Nav Sadhana Kala Kendra](/wiki/Nav_Sadhana_Kala_Kendra "Nav Sadhana Kala Kendra"), [Sampurnanand Sanskrit University](/wiki/Sampurnanand_Sanskrit_University "Sampurnanand Sanskrit University") and [Sri Agrasen Kanya P.G. College](/wiki/Sri_Agrasen_Kanya_P.G._College "Sri Agrasen Kanya P.G. College"). Various engineering colleges have been established in the outskirts of the city. Other notable universities and colleges include [Institute of Medical Sciences](/wiki/Institute_of_Medical_Sciences,_Banaras_Hindu_University "Institute of Medical Sciences, Banaras Hindu University"), [Sampurnanand Sanskrit Vishwavidyalaya](/wiki/Sampurnanand_Sanskrit_Vishwavidyalaya "Sampurnanand Sanskrit Vishwavidyalaya"), [Central Institute of Higher Tibetan Studies](/wiki/Central_Institute_of_Higher_Tibetan_Studies "Central Institute of Higher Tibetan Studies"), and [Harish Chandra Postgraduate College](/wiki/Harish_Chandra_Postgraduate_College "Harish Chandra Postgraduate College"). Some research oriented institutes were also established by the government such as International Rice Research Institute (IRRI),[[224]](#cite_note-229) [Indian Institute of Vegetable Research](/wiki/Indian_Council_of_Agricultural_Research "Indian Council of Agricultural Research")[[225]](#cite_note-230) and [National Seed Research and Training Centre](/wiki/Indian_Council_of_Agricultural_Research "Indian Council of Agricultural Research").[[226]](#cite_note-231)

[![Indian Institute of Technology in Varanasi](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Dept_of_Electrical_Engineering_IIT-BHU.jpg/250px-Dept_of_Electrical_Engineering_IIT-BHU.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dept_of_Electrical_Engineering_IIT-BHU.jpg)

[Indian Institute of Technology](/wiki/Indian_Institute_of_Technology_(BHU)_Varanasi "Indian Institute of Technology (BHU) Varanasi") in Varanasi

Varanasi also has three [Kendriya Vidyalaya](/wiki/Kendriya_Vidyalaya "Kendriya Vidyalaya"). Among them [Kendriya Vidyalaya BHU](/wiki/Kendriya_Vidyalaya_BHU "Kendriya Vidyalaya BHU") holds the regional office of Varanasi Region of KVS and is seat of deputy commissioner. Kendriya Vidyalaya BHU is also accredited by the [British Council](/wiki/British_Council "British Council"). Other KVs are Kendriya Vidyalaya 39 GTC and Kendriya Vidyalaya DLW.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[St. Joseph's Convent School](/w/index.php?title=St._Joseph%27s_Convent_School,_Varanasi&action=edit&redlink=1 "St. Joseph's Convent School, Varanasi (page does not exist)"), in Shivpur, Varanasi, was established by the Sisters of Our Lady of Providence of France as a Catholic (Christian) minority institution with the approval of the [Government of Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Government_of_Uttar_Pradesh "Government of Uttar Pradesh"). It is an autonomous organisation under the diocese of the Bishop of Varanasi. It provides education not only to the Catholic Christian children, but also to others who abide by its rules.[[227]](#cite_note-232)

Another important institution is the [Central Hindu School](/wiki/Central_Hindu_School "Central Hindu School") in Kamachha. This was established by [Annie Besant](/wiki/Annie_Besant "Annie Besant") in July 1898 with the objective of imparting secular education. It is affiliated to the [Central Board of Secondary Education](/wiki/Central_Board_of_Secondary_Education "Central Board of Secondary Education") and is open to students of all cultures.[[228]](#cite_note-233)[[229]](#cite_note-234)

Schools in Varanasi are affiliated with the [Indian Certificate of Secondary Education](/wiki/Indian_Certificate_of_Secondary_Education "Indian Certificate of Secondary Education") (ICSE), the CBSE, or the [Uttar Pradesh Board of Technical Education](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh_Board_of_Technical_Education "Uttar Pradesh Board of Technical Education") (U.P Board). The overall "state of education in Varanasi is ... not good."[[230]](#cite_note-sc-235) Schools in Varanasi vary widely in quality, with private schools outperforming government schools.[[230]](#cite_note-sc-235) In government schools, many teachers fail to come to class or to teach children.[[230]](#cite_note-sc-235) Some government schools lack basic equipment, such as blackboards and sufficient desks and chairs for all students.[[230]](#cite_note-sc-235) Private schools vary in quality, with the most expensive conducting lessons in English (seen as a key to children's success) and having computers in classrooms.[[230]](#cite_note-sc-235) Pupils attending the more expensive private schools, tended to come from upper-class families.[[230]](#cite_note-sc-235) Lower-cost private schools attracted children from lower-income families or those lower-income families with higher education aspirations.[[230]](#cite_note-sc-235) Government schools tend to serve lower-class children with lower education aspirations.[[230]](#cite_note-sc-235)

## Media

Main article: [Films shot in Varanasi](/wiki/List_of_films_shot_in_Varanasi "List of films shot in Varanasi")

Varanasi caters a lot of [shooting from different film industries](/wiki/List_of_films_shot_in_Varanasi "List of films shot in Varanasi") in India.[[231]](#cite_note-236) The temple town has emerged as a hub to [Hindi film industry](/wiki/Hindi_film_industry "Hindi film industry") and [South film industry](/wiki/Cinema_of_South_India "Cinema of South India").[[232]](#cite_note-237) Also, a chunk of Bhojpuri movies are shot in the city.[[233]](#cite_note-238) A few Bollywood movies that were shot, include [Gangs of Wasseypur](/wiki/Gangs_of_Wasseypur_%E2%80%93_Part_1 "Gangs of Wasseypur – Part 1"), [Masaan](/wiki/Masaan "Masaan"), [Raanjhanaa](/wiki/Raanjhanaa "Raanjhanaa"), [Piku](/wiki/Piku "Piku"), [Shubh Mangal Zyada Saavdhan](/wiki/Shubh_Mangal_Zyada_Saavdhan "Shubh Mangal Zyada Saavdhan") and [Super 30](/wiki/Super_30 "Super 30").[[234]](#cite_note-239)[[235]](#cite_note-240)[[236]](#cite_note-241)[[237]](#cite_note-242)[[238]](#cite_note-243) Some parts of the Hollywood movie [The Curious Case of Benjamin Button](/wiki/The_Curious_Case_of_Benjamin_Button_(film) "The Curious Case of Benjamin Button (film)") were also shot.[[239]](#cite_note-244) Web series such as [Mirzapur](/wiki/Mirzapur_(TV_series) "Mirzapur (TV series)") and [Asur](/wiki/Asur_(web_series) "Asur (web series)") were also shot in temple town.[[240]](#cite_note-245)[[241]](#cite_note-246)

Newspapers are widely available in Hindi and English. *[Aj](/wiki/Aj_(newspaper) "Aj (newspaper)")*, Hindi newspaper was established in 1920 in Varanasi.[[242]](#cite_note-About_us-247) Some publishers in the city are:

* *[Dainik Jagran](/wiki/Dainik_Jagran "Dainik Jagran")*[[243]](#cite_note-248)
* *[Hindustan](/wiki/Hindustan_(newspaper) "Hindustan (newspaper)")*[[244]](#cite_note-249)
* *[Amar Ujala](/wiki/Amar_Ujala "Amar Ujala")*[[245]](#cite_note-250)
* *Jansandesh Times*[[246]](#cite_note-251)
* *[Rajasthan Patrika](/wiki/Rajasthan_Patrika "Rajasthan Patrika")*[[247]](#cite_note-252)
* *[Aj](/wiki/Aj_(newspaper) "Aj (newspaper)")*[[242]](#cite_note-About_us-247)
* *[The Times of India](/wiki/The_Times_of_India "The Times of India")*[[248]](#cite_note-253)
* *[Hindustan Times](/wiki/Hindustan_Times "Hindustan Times")*[[249]](#cite_note-254)

The city also hosts a Doordarshan Kendra, which was established in 1984 by Prime Minister [Indira Gandhi](/wiki/Indira_Gandhi "Indira Gandhi"). In 1998, [Doordarshan](/wiki/Doordarshan "Doordarshan") studio was setup.[[250]](#cite_note-255)

FM/AM Stations available in the city are:[[251]](#cite_note-256)[[252]](#cite_note-257)[[253]](#cite_note-258)

* [Radio City](/wiki/Radio_City_(Indian_radio_station) "Radio City (Indian radio station)") 91.9 MHz
* [Red FM](/wiki/Red_FM_93.5 "Red FM 93.5") 93.5 MHz
* [BIG FM](/wiki/BIG_FM_92.7 "BIG FM 92.7") 95.0 MHz
* [Radio Mirchi](/wiki/Radio_Mirchi "Radio Mirchi") 98.3 MHz
* Radio Sunbeam 90.4 MHz
* [AIR Vividh Bharati](/wiki/Vividh_Bharati "Vividh Bharati") 100.6 MHz
* [Gyan Vani](/wiki/Gyan_Vani "Gyan Vani") 105.6 MHz
* AIR Varanasi 1242 AM

Mobile apps such as "InVaranasi", "Varanasi" and "LiveVNS" provide a wide range of information related to travel and local news.[[254]](#cite_note-259)[[255]](#cite_note-260)[[256]](#cite_note-261)

## Sport

[Basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), and [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") are popular sports in Varanasi.[[257]](#cite_note-262) The main stadium in the city is the [Dr Sampurnanda Stadium](/wiki/Dr_Sampurnanda_Stadium "Dr Sampurnanda Stadium") (Sigra Stadium), where first-class cricket matches are held.[[258]](#cite_note-263) The city also caters an AstroTurf hockey stadium named, Dr. Bheemrao Ambedker National Hockey Stadium.[[259]](#cite_note-264)

The Department of Physical Education, [Faculty of Arts](/wiki/Faculty_of_Arts,_Banaras_Hindu_University "Faculty of Arts, Banaras Hindu University") of [BHU](/wiki/Banaras_Hindu_University "Banaras Hindu University") offers diploma courses in Sports Management, Sports Physiotherapy, Sports Psychology and Sports Journalism.[[260]](#cite_note-265) Also, BHU caters sports complexes including badminton court, tennis court, swimming pool and [amphitheater](/wiki/Amphitheater "Amphitheater").[[261]](#cite_note-266)

[Gymnastics](/wiki/Gymnastics "Gymnastics") is also popular in Varanasi, and many Indian girls practise outdoors at the ghats in the mornings which hosts [akhadas](/wiki/Pehlwani "Pehlwani"), where "morning exercise, a dip in the Ganges and a visit to Lord [Hanuman](/wiki/Hanuman "Hanuman")" forms a daily ritual.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETiwari201047-267) Despite concerns regarding water quality, two swimming clubs offer swimming lessons in the Ganges.[[263]](#cite_note-268)

The Varanasi District Chess Sports Association (VDCSA) is based in Varanasi, affiliated to the regional Uttar Pradesh Chess Sports Association (UPCSA).[[264]](#cite_note-269)

## Transport

Within the city mobility is provided by taxis, rickshaws, cycle rickshaws, and three-wheelers, but with certain restrictions in the old town area of the city.[[265]](#cite_note-reach-270)

### Air transport

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Vns_.jpg/250px-Vns_.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vns_.jpg)

The [Lal Bahadur Shastri International Airport](/wiki/Lal_Bahadur_Shastri_International_Airport "Lal Bahadur Shastri International Airport") is the main airport that serves Varanasi.

Varanasi is served by [Lal Bahadur Shastri International Airport](/wiki/Lal_Bahadur_Shastri_International_Airport "Lal Bahadur Shastri International Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): **VNS**, [ICAO](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code"): **VEBN**), which is approximately 26 km (16 mi) from the city centre in [Babatpur](/wiki/Babatpur "Babatpur").[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitra2002195-271) The airport's new terminal was inaugurated in 2010, and it was granted international airport status on 4 October 2012.[[267]](#cite_note-272)

### Railways

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Varanasi_Railway_Station.JPG/250px-Varanasi_Railway_Station.JPG)](/wiki/File:Varanasi_Railway_Station.JPG)

[Varanasi Junction](/wiki/Varanasi_Junction "Varanasi Junction"), is the main railway station which serves Varanasi.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Manduadih_Railway_Station_at_night.jpg/250px-Manduadih_Railway_Station_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manduadih_Railway_Station_at_night.jpg)

Banaras railway station at night

[Varanasi Junction](/wiki/Varanasi_Junction_railway_station "Varanasi Junction railway station"), commonly known as Varanasi Cantt Railway Station, is the city's largest railway station. More than 360,000 passengers and 240 trains pass through each day.[[268]](#cite_note-273) [Banaras railway station](/wiki/Banaras_railway_station "Banaras railway station") is also a Terminal station of Varanasi. Because of huge rush at Varanasi Junction, the railway station was developed as a high facilitated terminal. [Varanasi City railway station](/wiki/Varanasi_City_railway_station "Varanasi City railway station") is also one of the railway stations in Varanasi district. It is located 4 kilometres (2.5 mi) north-east of Varanasi Junction railway station. It serves as Terminal station because of heavy rush at Varanasi Junction. [Pandit Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Junction railway station](/wiki/Pandit_Deen_Dayal_Upadhyaya_Junction_railway_station "Pandit Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Junction railway station") is also the important station in Varanasi suburban.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some important express trains operating from the Varanasi Junction railway station and [Banaras railway station](/wiki/Banaras_railway_station "Banaras railway station") are: [Shiv Ganga Express](/wiki/Shiv_Ganga_Express "Shiv Ganga Express") runs between New Delhi Junction and Manduadih station while [Mahamana Express](/wiki/Mahamana_Express "Mahamana Express") runs between Varanasi junction and New Delhi Junction; the [Udhna Varanasi Express](/wiki/Udhna_Varanasi_Express "Udhna Varanasi Express") that runs between Udhna (Surat) junction and Varanasi, a distance of 1,398 kilometres (869 mi);[[269]](#cite_note-274) the [Kashi Vishwanath Express](/wiki/Kashi_Vishwanath_Express "Kashi Vishwanath Express") that runs between Varanasi and [New Delhi railway station](/wiki/New_Delhi_railway_station "New Delhi railway station");[[270]](#cite_note-275) the Kanpur Varanasi InterCity express, also called Varuna express, which runs over a distance of 355 kilometres (221 mi) and connects with [Lucknow](/wiki/Lucknow "Lucknow") (the capital city of Uttar Pradesh) and [Varanasi](/wiki/Kanpur "Kanpur");[[271]](#cite_note-276) and the [Sabarmati Express](/wiki/Sabarmati_Express "Sabarmati Express") which runs between Varanasi and Ahmedabad. [Vande Bharat Express](/wiki/Vande_Bharat_Express "Vande Bharat Express"), a [semi-high speed](/wiki/Higher-speed_rail "Higher-speed rail") train was launched in the month of February in 2019 in the Delhi-Varanasi route.[[272]](#cite_note-277) The train reduced the time travel between the two cities by 15 per cent as compared to the [Shatabdi Express](/wiki/Shatabdi_Express "Shatabdi Express").[[273]](#cite_note-278)

### Ropeway

[Kashi ropeway](/wiki/Kashi_ropeway "Kashi ropeway") is under construction since 2023. It will be 3.75 kilometres (2.33 mi) long and will have a maximum capacity of 3000 [passengers per hour per direction](/wiki/Passengers_per_hour_per_direction "Passengers per hour per direction").[[276]](#cite_note-281)[[113]](#cite_note-Department_of_Home_and_Confidential,_Government_of_Uttar_Pradesh-118)[[114]](#cite_note-Department_of_Appointments_and_Personnel,_Government_of_Uttar_Pradesh-119)[[277]](#cite_note-282) It will cover the cantonment area to Godowlia, which will reduce travel time from 45 minutes to around 15 minutes.[[278]](#cite_note-283)

### Roads

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Varanasi_Ring_Road_Phase_1.jpg/250px-Varanasi_Ring_Road_Phase_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Varanasi_Ring_Road_Phase_1.jpg)

Ring Road Phase I

[Auto rickshaws](/wiki/Auto_rickshaw "Auto rickshaw") and [E-rickshaws](/wiki/Electric_rickshaw "Electric rickshaw") are the most widely available forms of public transport in the old city.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBruynBainAllardice2010467-284) In the outer regions of the city, taxis are available.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBruynBainAllardice2010467-284) Daily commuters prefer city buses, which operate on specific routes of urban and suburban areas. The city buses are operated by Varanasi City Transport Service Limited.[[280]](#cite_note-285) Nearly, 120 buses are operated by Varanasi City Transport Service Limited.[[281]](#cite_note-286)

The heavy traffic of the city is monitored through Integrated Traffic Management System. The smart traffic management system equips the city with automatic signal control system, separate signal system for pedestrians, traffic management centre at state level, area traffic control system, corridor management and dynamic traffic indicators for smooth movement of traffic.[[287]](#cite_note-292) Varanasi Traffic Police keeps an eye through Smart Command and Control Centre.[[288]](#cite_note-293)[[289]](#cite_note-294)

### Inland waterways

[National Waterway 1](/wiki/National_Waterway_1 "National Waterway 1") passes through Varanasi. In 2018, a new inland port was established on the banks of Ganges River.[[290]](#cite_note-295) The [Multi-Modal Terminal](/wiki/Varanasi_Multi-Modal_Terminal "Varanasi Multi-Modal Terminal") is designed to handle 1.26 million metric tons of cargo every year and covers an area of 34 hectares.[[291]](#cite_note-296) Nearly, ₹170 crore was invested by the government to set up an inland port.[[292]](#cite_note-297) Maersk started its container service in 2019 by moving 16 containers on [NW-1](/wiki/National_Waterway_1 "National Waterway 1") from Varanasi to Kolkata. The port also catered PepsiCo, IFFCO Fertilizers, Emami Agrotech and Dabur for cargo movement.[[293]](#cite_note-298)

## Projects

Due to growing population and industrial demands, the city is being implanted with several infrastructural projects.[[294]](#cite_note-299) In fiscal year 2014–18, the city was awarded with projects worth ₹30,000 crore.[[295]](#cite_note-300) The city is being invested by both private and public players in different sectors.[[296]](#cite_note-301)

### Metro

Main article: [Varanasi Metro](/wiki/Varanasi_Metro "Varanasi Metro")

The [Varanasi Metro](/wiki/Varanasi_Metro "Varanasi Metro") is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") proposed for Varanasi. The proposed system consists of two lines, spanning from [BHEL](/wiki/Bharat_Heavy_Electricals "Bharat Heavy Electricals") to [Banaras Hindu University](/wiki/Banaras_Hindu_University "Banaras Hindu University") (19.35 kilometres (12.02 mi)) and Benia Bagh to [Sarnath](/wiki/Sarnath "Sarnath") (9.885 kilometres (6.142 mi)). The feasibility study of the project was done by [RITES](/wiki/RITES "RITES") and was completed in June 2015. There will be 26 stations, including 20 underground and six elevated on the two lines, which includes total length of 29.235 kilometres (18.166 mi) consisting of 23.467 kilometres (14.582 mi) underground, while 5.768 kilometres (3.584 mi) will be elevated.[[315]](#cite_note-320)[[316]](#cite_note-321)[[317]](#cite_note-322)[[318]](#cite_note-323) The total estimated completion cost for construction of Varanasi Metro is estimated to be ₹13,133 crore (US$1.6 billion).[[319]](#cite_note-324)

    * Singh, Ravi Nandan (2022). *Dead in Banaras: An Ethnography of Funeral Travelling*. Oxford, UK: Oxford University Press. "The present-day Banaras, at first sign, is a new place. Rightly so, the baton must then pass on to an all new chronicling of the place. Yet, a connecting link, as always, may come into play, between the book's time and other times of Banaras. Let me give an example of what such a connection might look like. Jonathan Parry (1994) in his classic *Death in Banaras* laments in the preface to the book that he could not incorporate the coming in of the electric crematorium in his descriptions of the funerary organization in Banaras. Two decades later into my fieldwork, I found that it is in part, the efficiency of the open-air, manual cremation that Parry so effectively captures in his book that explains how a promising symbol of industrial modernity, the electric crematorium, falls short of the typecast. In the years between his book and my fieldwork, the electric crematorium sat lonely and was sparingly used against the cheer of the always-on, busy, manual pyres whose flames continue to dot the scene of the ghats in a contrasting relief. In this above sense, I believe, Parry already provides us a portrait of the electric crematorium's social imaginary in Banaras. The question of the shift from wooden pyres to the electric crematorium is then not about competing technologies but that of ethics with which the dead are tended to amidst the assemblies of funeral travellers."

The history of cremation in India is far more than the history of traditional rites and practices that it is conventionally taken to be—if tradition is assumed to mean "timeless" custom and immutable belief. "'Varanasi … is the city where Hindus and Muslims … are interwoven like threads as in the lovely silk saris for which Kashi (Varanasi) is so famous for (Puniyani, 2006).'(quoted) Varanasi is most often represented as a sacred Hindu pilgrimage centre (see Eck, 1983), as its social and cultural urban spaces have been often examined through the imagined and lived realities of Hinduism (Hertel and Humes, 1993; Parry, 1994; Singh and Rana, 2002). Modi also targeted the important Muslim community of sari weavers in the district of Lallapura. Varanasi's silk wedding saris, lavishly designed and interwoven with gold thread, are much sought after by Indians from across the country and from overseas, and Modi promised to help the weavers acquire modern technology, quality raw materials and better marketing skills so they could compete with Chinese clothing manufacturers. 'The weavers of Varanasi are an integral part of the city's history', he wrote in a blog post on his nomination day. 'It is my resolve to ensure that they stand on their own feet with pride and their future generations have a bright future.' (pp. 51–52) An earlier great poet, son of Varanasi and symbol of north India's syncretic traditions, was the fifteenth-century Kabir, whose pithy lines in Hindi are still much quoted today by Indians over social media. But he famously mocked the priesthoods and the rituals of both Muslims and Hindus, even to the extent of deliberately leaving holy Varanasi to die in an obscure town, when most north Indians would be heading in the other direction and yearning to expire within the boundaries of the city to find salvation. 29. **[^](#cite_ref-bourke-burke-benares_34-0)** Bourke, Richard (8 September 2015), [*Empire and Revolution: The Political Life of Edmund Burke*](https://books.google.com/books?id=E6eeDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA843), Princeton University Press, p. 843, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780691145112](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780691145112 "Special:BookSources/9780691145112"), "Since 1724 Awadh, "in extent about the size of England," had enjoyed effective autonomy as a quasi-independent province within the Mughal Empire. It came to terms with the British after the Battle of Buxar in 1764, finally signing up to the Treaty of Benares in 1773. This imposed on the Wazir of Awadh, Shuja-ud-Daula, the obligation to accept troops stationed in his territory while paying a subsidy to the British for the privilege. The Wazir's successor, Asaf-ud-Daula, agreed to increase this subsidy under the Treaty of Faizabad in 1775, at the same time ceding Benares to the Company."
30. **[^](#cite_ref-markovitz-benares_35-0)** Markovitz, Claude (24 September 2004), ["Birth of the British Empire in India (1765–1818)"](https://books.google.com/books?id=r2OKvG5wbaAC&pg=PA254), in Markovits, Claude (ed.), *A History of Modern India, 1480–1950*, Anthem Press, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9781843311522](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9781843311522 "Special:BookSources/9781843311522"), "In the face of the hostility of all the dynamic forces of the country, the threat of the Marathas looming large on the frontiers, Asaf could count henceforth only on the help of the English, who exerted themselves to use this tumultuous situation to the full. In exchange for their military protection, the *nawab* granted them as early as 1775 the control of the region of Benares, in addition to a substantial increase in indemnity."
31. **[^](#cite_ref-bayly-benares-division_36-0)** [Bayly, Christopher](/wiki/Christopher_Bayly "Christopher Bayly") (1983), [*Rulers, townsmen, and bazaars: north Indian society in the age of British expansion, 1770–1870*](https://archive.org/details/rulerstownsmenba0000bayl/page/n15/mode/2up?), Cambridge University Press, pp. xii–xiii, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780521229326](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780521229326 "Special:BookSources/9780521229326"), "In 1801 a large area of Awadh situated in the Doab and Rohilkhand were ceded to the British. It was added to districts conquered from the Marathas in 1803–4 around Delhi and Agra to form the 'Conquered and Ceded Provinces' of the British Bengal Presidency. The term 'Western Provinces' and later 'North-Western Provinces' came into gradual use to describe this area and the adjoining Benares Division; ... In 1856 the remaining 'Reserved Dominions' of the ruler of Awadh were annexed to become the British Province of Oudh under a Chief Commissioner. In 1901 the two provinces were amalgamated to become the United Province of Agra and Oudh."
